title: Dog Small Lump at Vaccination Site: What to Do | Vet Triage Guide
meta_desc: Found a small lump after your dog's shots? Learn why it happens, why it is usually low urgency, and how to monitor it at home.
content_html:
What is this lump?

It is very common for dogs to develop a small, firm, and painless lump at the injection site following a vaccination. This is typically a localized inflammatory reaction as your dog's immune system processes the vaccine. These bumps are usually harmless and are simply a sign that the body is responding to the preventative care.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for a small, non-painful lump at a vaccination site is Low. In most cases, these lumps resolve on their own within a few weeks without any medical intervention. You should only seek urgent care if your dog develops severe symptoms like facial swelling, persistent vomiting, or difficulty breathing, which could indicate an immediate allergic reaction.

Why taking a photo helps

Taking a clear photo of the lump today is a vital part of the triage process. By placing a common object, such as a coin or a ruler, next to the bump for scale, you create a visual baseline. This allows you to monitor the site accurately over time and provides your veterinarian with helpful data if the lump changes in size, shape, or color.

faqs.question: How long will the lump stay there?
faqs.answer: Most post-vaccination lumps disappear on their own within two to six weeks.

faqs.question: Should I apply heat or ice to the bump?
faqs.answer: Generally, it is best to leave the area alone. Avoid massaging or applying compresses unless specifically instructed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: What is the '3-2-1 rule' for vaccine lumps?
faqs.answer: Veterinarians often suggest the 3-2-1 rule: seek a check-up if the lump persists for more than 3 months, is larger than 2 centimeters, or is increasing in size 1 month after the injection.
